what_it_does: This file is a generated JSON registry that catalogs the files and folders of the design repository. It groups every item into directory-based clusters, classifies each by file type, and records metadata such as size, depth, modification time, order, and spine flags. Despite its name, it contains no edges or references, so it functions as a typed census or address book rather than a connected graph.
observations: Meta reports 669 addresses, 554 files, 17 folders, 0 edges, 0 ghosts, and 0 spine items.
A types map enumerates file extensions with icons and counts, including separate entries for jpg and jpeg.
The clusters array contains 17 clusters, with review being the largest at 266 files and ~63 MB, mostly images.
The pieces cluster holds 85 files, largely paired v1.html and v2.html variants of design experiments.
The docs cluster contains 48 Markdown documents organized into campaigns, command, dev, derived, letters, loop, and analysis subfolders.
The surface cluster includes runtime JavaScript, room scripts, CSS, and a viewer scaffold.
The registry cluster lists this file itself plus other registry JSON files and a wayfinder markdown file.
Every node has refs:0, out:0, summary:"", and spine:false, making the relationship fields uniform and empty.
edges and spine arrays at the root are both empty.
The file includes a __pycache__ cluster with a single .pyc build artifact.
standouts: The review cluster dominates the repository by both file count and byte size, containing many ingested pitch-deck and mockup images.
pieces and review share matching variant names such as var-1-trizone, test-A-cool-precise, and gen3-atoms, suggesting a variant-driven design workflow.
The file calls itself a graph but currently has no edges, references, summaries, or spine entries.
All nodes use the same schema, with id as a relative path and label as the basename.
The registry cluster is self-referential, listing graph.json and other registry artifacts.
conventions: Clusters group files by parent directory.
Each node uses a consistent schema: id, label, type, type_label, icon, refs, bytes, lines, depth, order, mtime, summary, spine, out.
File types are normalized and decorated with icon and human-readable type_label.
Order and depth fields are provided for every node, likely for layout or traversal sequencing.
Variant naming follows patterns like var-NN-descriptor, test-X-descriptor, genN-descriptor, app-descriptor, and scr-descriptor.
concerns: edges and refs are zero everywhere, so the graph structure is not yet realized.
summary fields are empty for every node, leaving no human-readable annotation.
Meta file_count (554) does not exactly match the sum of cluster file_count values (553).
Text files have lines:null, so the registry does not capture line counts.
Build artifacts (__pycache__) are included in the registry.
jpg and jpeg are treated as separate types even though they represent the same image format.
notes: This file is a generated directory census dressed as a graph: it clusters the repo by folder, tags every file with type, size, depth, and order, but leaves all relationship fields empty.
questions: What distinguishes an address from a file or folder in the meta counts?
Are edges and spine intended to be populated later, or is this registry meant to stay a flat index?
How is the order value assigned to each node?
Why are summary and refs uniformly empty?
Why is __pycache__ included as a cluster?
What do spine and out signify in this schema?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is a registry graph, but actual content is a flat typed census with no relationships; it succeeds as a registry and fails as a graph.
intention_signals: The file appears to be trying to create a machine-readable map of the entire design repository, establishing a common node schema that can later support graph-based navigation and relationship discovery across code, design, and documentation substrates.
novelty: The schema combines file-system inventory with design-system semantics by encoding variant names and ingest sources directly in node ids, while reserving empty slots for edges, spine, and summaries that could be filled by downstream tooling.
contribution: It provides a unified, addressable inventory of all repository files grouped by directory, with a consistent per-node metadata schema and explicit type classification. A fused system could use it as a lookup table and scaffold for discovering relationships, even though the relationships themselves are not yet present.
summary_for_embedding: A generated JSON registry graph that catalogs 554 files across 17 clusters in the counterpart-design repository, classifying each file by type, size, depth, and order, while leaving edges, references, summaries, and spine relationships unpopulated.
intention_for_embedding: To serve as a machine-readable map and scaffold for navigating the design repository and eventually wiring relationships between its code, design, and documentation substrates.
